issues.current_text: the hash code of `x`, computed by mixing the class name and each product element
issues.problem: For empty case classes (productArity == 0) the implementation returns just the class name's hashCode without any mixing or finalization, so the phrase 'mixing the class name and each product element' is slightly inaccurate at the boundary.
issues.suggestion: the hash code of `x`, derived from the class name and each product element

issues.current_text: the MurmurHash3 hash of the string's characters
issues.problem: Slightly tautological in a class named MurmurHash3 and doesn't convey what's distinctive (the characters are packed two-at-a-time into 32-bit blocks before mixing).
issues.suggestion: the hash of `str`, computed by packing successive pairs of UTF-16 chars into 32-bit blocks and finalizing with the string length

issues.current_text: a hash code that depends on element order, matching `rangeHash` when `xs` forms an arithmetic progression
issues.problem: The `rangeHash` compatibility only applies when `xs` has at least 3 elements and a non-zero stride (the implementation falls back to plain mixing for 0- or 1-element inputs and for zero `rangeDiff`); the current wording implies it always matches for any arithmetic progression.
issues.suggestion: a hash code that depends on element order, matching `rangeHash` when `xs` has at least three elements and forms an arithmetic progression with non-zero step

issues.current_text: the hash of `a`, compatible with `rangeHash` when the elements form an arithmetic progression
issues.problem: Same as `orderedHash`: the range-hash fast path requires at least 3 elements with non-zero stride, so the unqualified 'arithmetic progression' wording is slightly broader than the actual behavior.
issues.suggestion: the hash of `a`, compatible with `rangeHash` when `a` has at least three elements forming an arithmetic progression with non-zero step

issues.current_text: the hash of `a`, compatible with `rangeHash` when the elements form an arithmetic progression
issues.problem: Same boundary issue as `arrayHash`/`orderedHash`: 0- or 1-element inputs and zero-stride sequences fall back to the generic path.
issues.suggestion: the hash of `a`, compatible with `rangeHash` when `a` has at least three elements forming an arithmetic progression with non-zero step

issues.current_text: the hash of `xs`, compatible with `rangeHash` when the elements form an arithmetic progression
issues.problem: Same caveat as the other range-aware hashers; the implementation only switches to `rangeHash` when `rangeState == 2`, i.e. with a non-degenerate arithmetic progression of sufficient length.
issues.suggestion: the hash of `xs`, compatible with `rangeHash` when `xs` has at least three elements forming an arithmetic progression with non-zero step

general_feedback: The newly filled-in @return descriptions are accurate, clear, consistent in style with the existing @param wording, and appropriately concise. The main area for refinement is the family of range-aware hashers (`orderedHash`, `arrayHash`, `indexedSeqHash`, `listHash`), whose `rangeHash` compatibility is conditioned on at least three elements and a non-zero stride — the current wording slightly overstates it. The `caseClassHash` and `stringHash` returns could be tightened for the empty-case-class boundary and to convey the packing strategy, respectively. No factual errors and no consistency issues with surrounding scaladoc.
